Right — the min-heap is the part that trips people up, so let me make it crystal clear **why a *min*-heap is correct for finding the *most* frequent.**

## The counter-intuitive bit

Your gut says: "I want the *largest* counts → use a *max*-heap." That works, but it's the *wrong tool* for top-**k**. Here's the logic:

> We don't want to *order* all items. We want to **keep k and reject the rest.**
> To reject, we need cheap access to the **current weakest** item — so we can kick it out.
> A **min-heap** puts the weakest (smallest freq) right on top → O(1) to find, O(log k) to evict.

So the min-heap isn't holding "the answer in order" — it's a **bouncer at a club with k spots.** Every new item walks up; if the club is full, the bouncer compares the newcomer against the *weakest person inside* (the heap top) and keeps the stronger one.

```
heap (min on top), k=2:

   top → (2, Kindle)      ← weakest inside, first to be evicted
         (3, iPhone15)

new item (1, EchoDot) arrives:
   push → size becomes 3 > k
   pop top → removes the SMALLEST = (1, EchoDot)   ✓ weakest leaves
```

## Min-heap vs max-heap — the real difference

| | Max-heap of ALL items | **Min-heap of size k** |
|---|---|---|
| What's on top | largest | **smallest (the one to evict)** |
| Heap size | d (all distinct) | **only k** |
| Time | O(n + d log d) | **O(n + d log k)** |
| Idea | sort everything, take k | **keep k, bounce the weakest** |

When k ≪ d (e.g. top 10 out of a million products), `log k` is tiny and `log d` is not — that's the whole win.

> 💬 How I'd say it out loud: "I use a *min*-heap even though I want the most frequent — because the job isn't to sort, it's to keep only k. The min-heap shows me the weakest of my current k on top, so when something better comes along I pop that weakest in log k. The k strongest naturally survive."

## The two bugs to self-flag

```python
heapq.heappush(min_heap, (freq, product))
if len(min_heap) > k:
    heapq.heappop(min_heap)   # ⚠️ pops the MIN — that's the point
```

1. **⚠️ Direction:** you pop the **min** (weakest). If you ever find yourself wanting to pop the max, you've confused yourself — stop and re-derive: "largest survive → smallest leaves → min-heap."
2. **⚠️ Drain order:** popping a min-heap at the end gives you **lowest-frequency first**, so `result.reverse()` to get most-frequent first.

Python only has `heapq` (a min-heap). If you genuinely wanted a max-heap you'd push `-freq` — but for top-k you *don't*, the min-heap is exactly right.

Want me to walk through the heap's internal array re-balancing (sift-up / sift-down) so you can explain *how* `heappush`/`heappop` stay O(log k), in case the interviewer probes the heap internals?
